text: To establish leverage and define a clear walk-away point, a negotiator must identify their {{c1::BATNA (Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement)}} before entering any talks.
explanation: Your BATNA is the course of action you will take if negotiations fail. Knowing your alternative prevents you from accepting unfavorable terms out of desperation and gives you confidence.
usage: • Example: Securing a job offer from Company B before negotiating salary details with Company A.
spanish: Para establecer influencia y definir un punto de retirada claro, un negociador debe identificar su BATNA (Mejor Alternativa a un Acuerdo Negociado) antes de iniciar cualquier conversación.

text: The overlap range between the buyer's maximum walk-away price and the seller's minimum walk-away price is known as the {{c1::ZOPA (Zone of Possible Agreement)}}.
explanation: If the buyer is willing to spend up to $100 and the seller is willing to accept down to $80, the ZOPA is $80-$100. If the buyer's maximum is lower than the seller's minimum, no ZOPA exists, and negotiation is pointless.
usage: • Example: Finding that a vendor's lowest acceptable contract value matches your budget limit.
spanish: El rango de superposición entre el precio máximo de salida del comprador y el precio mínimo de salida del vendedor se conoce como ZOPA (Zona de Posible Acuerdo).

text: By making the first offer, a negotiator exploits the cognitive bias known as {{c1::Anchoring}}, which disproportionately influences all subsequent bids.
explanation: The human brain is biased toward using the first piece of information offered as a reference point. A high initial anchor pulls the counter-offers upward, defining the center of gravity of the negotiation.
usage: • Actionable Advice: When negotiating, make an ambitious but defensible opening offer to set the anchor in your favor.
spanish: Al hacer la primera oferta, un negociador explota el sesgo cognitivo conocido como Anclaje, el cual influye desproporcionadamente en todas las ofertas posteriores.

text: The absolute maximum or minimum value a negotiator is willing to accept before walking away is called their {{c1::Reservation Price}}.
explanation: Unlike target prices, the reservation price is a hard limit. It is directly derived from your BATNA. Crossing this price means the negotiation is worse than walking away.
usage: • Example: Deciding beforehand that you will not accept any salary package below $85k, based on your current job's value.
spanish: El valor absoluto máximo o mínimo que un negociador está dispuesto a aceptar antes de retirarse se llama su Precio de Reserva.

text: To move past deadlocks, principled negotiation recommends that parties separate the people from the problem and focus on {{c1::interests (why they want it)}} rather than {{c2::positions (what they want)}}.
explanation: Positions are concrete demands (e.g., 'We want 10% more pay'). Interests are the underlying needs (e.g., 'We need to cover rising inflation costs'). Focusing on interests allows for creative win-win solutions.
usage: • Example: Instead of arguing over office space allocation, uncovering that one team needs quiet zones while the other needs collaboration space.
spanish: Para superar los puntos muertos, la negociación basada en principios recomienda que las partes separen a las personas del problema y se concentren en los intereses (por qué lo quieren) en lugar de las posiciones (qué quieren).

text: Chris Voss defines {{c1::Tactical Empathy}} as the active effort to understand the counterpart's mindset and feelings, and vocally reflecting it to make them feel heard.
explanation: Tactical empathy is not agreement or compassion; it is analytical intelligence. By demonstrating that you understand their worldview and fears, you disarm their defensive neurochemistry and open them to persuasion.
usage: • Actionable Advice: Use soft tone and neutral statements to show you understand their constraints (e.g., 'It seems you are under a lot of pressure to meet this deadline').
spanish: Chris Voss define la Empatía Táctica como el esfuerzo activo por comprender la mentalidad y los sentimientos del interlocutor, y reflejarlos verbalmente para que se sienta escuchado.

text: To neutralize negative emotions in a hostile counterpart, you perform {{c1::Labeling}}, naming the emotion using neutral phrases like 'It sounds like...' or 'It seems like...'.
explanation: Labeling triggers the cognitive part of the counterpart's brain (prefrontal cortex), pulling power away from the emotional amygdala. You must never use 'I hear...' or 'I think...', as it draws focus back to you.
usage: • Example: Saying 'It seems like you feel this agreement is unfair' instead of 'Why are you angry with me?'
spanish: Para neutralizar las emociones negativas en un interlocutor hostil, realizas el Etiquetado, nombrando la emoción usando frases neutras como 'Parece que...' o 'Suena como...'.

text: In high-stakes conflict resolution, the negotiator practices Mirroring by repeating the {{c1::last one to three words}} (or key words) of what the other person just said.
explanation: Mirroring feels natural to the speaker and creates rapport. It prompts the other person to elaborate and explain their thoughts further without the negotiator having to ask probing questions.
usage: • Example: Counterpart: 'We just can't accept this schedule.' Negotiator: 'Accept this schedule?' Counterpart: 'Yes, because our QA team is short-staffed this month.'
spanish: En la resolución de conflictos de alto nivel, el negociador practica el Reflejo repitiendo las últimas una a tres palabras (o palabras clave) de lo que la otra persona acaba de decir.

text: Before presenting a difficult request, a negotiator performs an {{c1::Accusations Audit}}, listing all the worst negative things the other side might think or say about them.
explanation: By calling out the negative perceptions yourself (e.g., 'You're going to think I'm greedy and ignoring our deal'), you pop the emotional bubble before they can use it. It makes them want to contradict your self-accusations.
usage: • Example: 'I know this will sound like we are completely unorganized and don't respect your team's time, but...'
spanish: Antes de presentar una solicitud difícil, un negociador realiza una Auditoría de Acusaciones, enumerando todas las peores cosas negativas que la otra parte podría pensar o decir sobre ellos.

text: To guide the opponent to solve your problem without causing defensiveness, you ask {{c1::Calibrated Questions}}, which are open-ended questions beginning with 'How' or 'What'.
explanation: These questions remove the aggressiveness of demands. By asking 'How am I supposed to do that?', you subtly force the counterpart to look at your constraints and design a solution for you.
usage: • Actionable Advice: Avoid 'Why' questions as they sound accusatory. Use 'What is the biggest challenge we face here?' or 'How can we solve this together?'
spanish: Para guiar al oponente a resolver tu problema sin provocar defensividad, formulas Preguntas Calibradas, que son preguntas abiertas que comienzan con 'Cómo' o 'Qué'.

text: Rather than pushing for a premature 'Yes', a negotiator understands that letting the counterpart say {{c1::'No'}} gives them a sense of safety and control, making them more open to compromise.
explanation: 'Yes' feels like a commitment, which makes people protective and cautious. 'No' feels like protection; once they say 'No', they relax and are ready to discuss details and constructive options.
usage: • Actionable Advice: Change 'Do you agree to this?' to 'Are you against this proposal?' or 'Is it a bad idea to review these numbers?'
spanish: En el contexto de la negociación, permitir que la contraparte diga 'No' les da una sensación de seguridad y control, haciéndolos más abiertos al compromiso.

text: The ultimate breakthrough in conflict resolution occurs when you summarize the counterpart's position so well that they exclaim {{c1::'That's right!'}}.
explanation: Saying 'You're right' is a defense mechanism to make you go away. Saying 'That's right' means they feel completely understood. This emotional connection shifts them from combatants to collaborators.
usage: • Actionable Advice: Summarize their situation, pain points, and feelings objectively until they confirm your summary with 'That's right!'.
spanish: El avance definitivo en la resolución de conflictos ocurre cuando resumes la posición de la contraparte tan bien que exclaman '¡Así es!' (o '¡Es correcto!').

text: In any high-stakes negotiation, the hidden pieces of information that completely change the deal's dynamic are referred to as {{c1::Black Swans}}.
explanation: Black Swans are details that you don't know you don't know (e.g., a competitor's bankruptcy, a personal relationship, or a hidden fear). Finding them requires deep listening and observing deviations from normal behavior.
usage: • Example: Discovering that a supplier's main constraint is not price, but a quarterly volume target they must hit by Friday.
spanish: En cualquier negociación de alto nivel, las piezas de información ocultas que cambian por completo la dinámica del acuerdo se conocen como Cisnes Negros.

text: To defuse passive-aggressive behavior in meetings, a leader avoids defensive replies and instead uses {{c1::non-defensive questioning}} to bring the subtext into the open.
explanation: Defending yourself or attacking back validates their hidden aggression. Asking 'It sounds like you have a concern about this rollout, what specific risk are you seeing?' forces them to either articulate a real problem or drop the behavior.
usage: • Example: Meeting snipe: 'Well, I hope we actually finish on time this time.' Response: 'It sounds like you feel our timeline is unrealistic. Let's look at the milestones.'
spanish: Para desactivar el comportamiento pasivo-agresivo en las reuniones, un líder evita las respuestas defensivas y en su lugar utiliza preguntas no defensivas para sacar a la luz el subtexto.

text: The Ackerman Model is a systematic offering sequence: starting at 65% of target, then offering {{c1::85%, 95%, and finally 100%}} with precise, non-round numbers and a non-monetary concession.
explanation: Using non-round numbers (e.g., $95,420 instead of $95,000) makes the number look calculated and absolute, rather than arbitrary. Decreasing increments signals that you are hitting your absolute limit, forcing the other side to accept.
usage: • Example: Buying services at a final bid of $12,385, accompanied by offering to write a positive public case study for their website.
spanish: El modelo Ackerman es una secuencia sistemática de ofertas: comenzando al 65% del objetivo, luego ofreciendo 85%, 95% y finalmente 100% con números precisos y no redondos, y una concesión no monetaria.
